name: Kami of Thunder
description: The Kami of Thunder, often associated with Raijin (also known as Kaminari-sama), is a powerful and fearsome figure in Japanese mythology. Raijin is depicted as a demonic-looking spirit, often surrounded by drums and summoning lightning and thunder. His origins are intertwined with Shinto beliefs about the natural world and the forces that govern it. He is responsible for bringing storms, rain, and the life-giving power of water to the land, but also capable of destruction and chaos. Legends say he was imprisoned by Sugawara no Michizane, a scholar deified as Tenjin, the kami of scholarship. Raijin's image is often paired with that of Fujin, the kami of wind, reflecting the interconnectedness of natural forces in Japanese cosmology. He is both revered and feared, embodying the duality of nature's power. He is sometimes considered a protector against evil spirits, as the sound of thunder is believed to ward off demons. The loud crash of thunder is attributed to Raijin beating his drums, creating the booming sound that echoes across the sky. His influence extends to agriculture, as rain is essential for crops, and his presence is a reminder of the power and unpredictability of the natural world. Raijin's visual representation includes wild, unkempt hair, fierce eyes, and a muscular physique, emphasizing his raw power and untamed nature. He is a vital part of the Japanese pantheon, representing the untamed power of storms and the importance of respecting the forces of nature.
subtitle: Master of Storms and Lightning

keyMyths.title: Raijin's Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: Raijin was captured and imprisoned by Sugawara no Michizane (Tenjin). This myth explains why Raijin is sometimes depicted as subservient to Tenjin, the kami of scholarship.

keyMyths.title: The Drumming of Thunder
keyMyths.description: Raijin creates thunder by beating his drums in the sky. Each strike of the drum is said to create a loud crash of thunder that can be heard across the land.
keyMyths.source: Oral tradition and Shinto religious texts

keyMyths.title: Raijin and Fujin
keyMyths.description: Raijin and Fujin often appear together, representing opposing yet complementary forces of nature. Raijin controls thunder and lightning, while Fujin controls the wind and storms.

sources.author: Donald Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, an early Japanese chronicle.
